description: A compact axe rebuilt for splitting boards and skulls, with a nail-pulling notch hammered into the back.
A long defensive pike made from a reinforced board and sharpened scrap blade.
Small sections of beaten sheet metal with holes punched for lacing. Heavy, ugly, and useful against teeth.
A length of chain with a taped handle and weighted end. Dangerous to the target and embarrassingly dangerous to the user.
A large knife fixed behind a crossguard made from scrap, meant to stop impaled things from sliding down the shaft.
A trimmed section of tire rubber and leather meant to reinforce the soles and toes of battered footwear.
Cut seals and cloth rings used to stop bottles and jugs rattling or leaking in improvised carriers.
A snapped garden trowel sharpened into a nasty little thrusting blade.
A small fixed blade lashed to a hardened shaft. Better reach, worse knife.
A square of tarp, canvas, and heavy cloth cut to reinforce coats, packs, and bedrolls.
A sharpened ceramic shard set into a tape-and-cloth handle. Sharp, brittle, and made from somebody else's kitchen floor.
A baton wrapped with chain links and padded under the grip.
A length of chain and hooked scrap fixed to a taped handle. It is dangerous to the user, loud to swing, and nasty when it lands.
A short length of chain wrapped with cloth at one end and wire at the other, ready for hafting or reinforcement.
A staff capped with a short chain wrap, giving it a jarring, bone-rattling head.
A battered cleaver lashed to a hardened shaft, halfway between kitchen accident and polearm.
Layered cloth circles packed with charcoal dust, meant for masks and improvised strainers.
A short sledge head and pipe handle made for smashing masonry and mistakes. It is too heavy for finesse, but finesse died early.
A crate lid bolted to a short handle. It blocks badly and hits better than expected.
A crowbar lashed to a hooked cutting strip, giving it a brutal chopping beak.
A light crowbar lashed and pinned to a pipe shaft. It pries poorly compared with the original tool, but it gives a survivor reach without throwing away the habit of forcing doors.
A cloth bag of crushed charcoal sorted for filters, fire kits, and odor control.
A medical crutch rebuilt around a spike and forearm strap. It reaches far and flexes badly.
A drilled chunk of curb stone wired to a pipe handle. Heavy, fragile, brutal.
Two pipes lashed around a wooden core. Heavy enough to hurt, balanced enough to train with.
A cloth-wrapped pack of charcoal, sand, and folded fabric prepared for masks or emergency filters.
A long wooden stake hardened over flame and wrapped for grip. Primitive, light, and better than bare hands.
A short two-handed ram made from pipe and wrapped padding. It is more tool than weapon until it lands.
A folded, cleaned panel of tarp that can become a poncho, bag liner, or rain catcher.
A thick shard of glass taped into a cloth grip. It is disposable, ugly, and very sharp until it is not.
Layers of denim, leather, plastic sheet, and tape meant to protect elbows, knees, and hands from broken windows.
A halligan bar extended with a pipe haft and sharpened pick end. Heavy, but excellent at insisting.
A straight shaft scraped, fire-hardened, and wrapped so it can become a spear, hook, or tool handle.
A metal crutch rebuilt into a hooked staff. Light enough to carry, annoying enough to survive with.
Layered cloth, plastic, and stuffing tied into compact rolls for cold-weather repairs.
Overlapping pieces of tin, plastic, and leather laced onto a flexible backing.
A plank faced with bent metal and protruding nails. Crude trauma on a stick.
A rake head reworked with long nails and wire. It claws at distance.
Short rods and bracing plates tied into a reusable frame section for heavy carrying rigs.
A reinforced strap with padding, buckles, and crude stitching. Good enough for a new bag or harness.
A heavy shard of sharpened metal with a taped grip. Short, ugly, and direct.
Layers of cloth, foam, and blanket scraps stitched into a roll of padding for crude armor and carrying rigs.
A pipe with a bent hook bolted near the head. Good for pulling shields, legs, and bad ideas closer.
A steel pipe weighted with a lump of scrap and wrapped with tape. It is ugly, loud, and tiring, but a clean hit can stop a corpse cold.
A short pipe handle with a triangular scrap point set across the knuckles.
A long pipe capped and wrapped for grip. It is a scavenger's quarterstaff: simple, durable, and easy to repair after a bad swing.
A pipe shaft with a flattened hook blade. Good reach, crude bite.
A noisy tin of nails, wire loops, washers, and screws sorted by someone who still believed in organization.
A thick padded panel made from blanket scraps and stitched cloth, useful as warmth or bite padding.
Wire and thin scrap twisted into a close-range cutting whip. Dangerous to everything, including you.
Three short rebar tines welded and wired to a shaft. It catches, jabs, and bends.
A long rebar point with a crude side hook for dragging corpses off balance.
A shorter, lighter rebar spike balanced more for throwing than fencing. It is crude, but it can punch into soft targets before they reach you.
A short length of rebar ground into a stabbing blade with a wrapped handle.
A length of rebar hammered and ground into a crude spear point, with cloth wrapped around the grip. It is heavier than a proper spear, but it keeps teeth and claws at arm's length.
A rescue-ish axe made from a hatchet head and hooked pry spike.
Strips of worn but usable rubber cut for boot soles and grip patches.
A broad scrap blade with a heavy spine and wrapped handle. It chops more than it cuts.
A bundled set of belts, webbing, torn pack straps, and buckles sorted for rebuilding wearable gear.
A long zipper cut from a torn bag or jacket with enough cloth left to sew it into something else.
A scrap knife with crude saw teeth along the spine. It is not elegant, but it keeps cutting.
A shovel blade filed into a crude chopping edge and given extra grip tape.
A curved strip of sheet metal with a wrapped handle and crude knuckle guard.
A hand-sized plate of flattened steel with drilled tie points for armor or tool guards.
A long pole fitted with a cutting plate, spike, and hook. It is too heavy for clean fencing but fine for desperate distance.
Door hinges and bent brackets hammered flat enough to reuse as folding joints or guard plates.
A cluster of screwdrivers wired around a short handle to make a nasty thrusting weapon.
A sorted handful of buckles, slides, and clips salvaged from ruined bags and belts.
A strip of scrap metal filed into a rough edge and wrapped for later mounting.
A long sheet-metal strip folded around a crude core and ground to a serviceable edge.
A knife lashed to a shortened handle for cramped hallways and doorways. It lacks the reach of a real spear, but it is much easier to carry through a broken house.
A short handle capped with heavy sockets and tape. It hits harder than it looks and looks bad enough already.
A quarterstaff capped with heavy sockets and wire. It turns scrap into reach.
A short baton drilled through with nails and wrapped in tape. It is compact, mean, and exactly the sort of thing someone makes after watching barricades fail.
Flat sticks, short boards, and cloth ties bundled for medical splints or structural reinforcement.
A narrow blade of filed scrap reinforced between wooden splints.
A layered strip of wood, cloth, and mesh that catches glass and wooden fragments before they reach skin.
A broken board split into a wedge point and reinforced with wire. It is more panic than craft, but the point is real.
A compact maul head with added pipe weight. Built to break bone and furniture.
A cut street sign sharpened and bolted to a shaft. Lightweight, wide, and mean.
A table leg, chain, and heavy fitting. Miserable to carry, hard to ignore.
A sticky wad of tarred cloth, ash, and glue for sealing tarp, boots, and rough containers.
A stiff sheet of cloth rubbed with oil, soot, and melted plastic. It sheds rain badly but better than bare fabric.
A restrained survivor spear made from a proper knife, a straight shaft, and careful bindings.
A short club studded with washers and bolts around the striking end.
A plank reinforced with wire, nails, and a chunk of metal near the striking end. It catches on clothing and bone, but it hits hard for something built on a shelter floor.
A bent security bar wrapped at one end. Dense, simple, and not subtle.
A jagged strip of saw blade mounted as a slashing weapon for desperate close work.
A flexible mesh of wire and punched metal useful for face guards, filters, and crude splinter protection.
A sledge head, pipe handle, and far too much tape. Subtlety left town before this was built.
A shallow box with wire mesh for sorting charcoal, ash, nails, and powdered debris.
A hammer with a reinforced handle, nail-pulling notch, wrapped grip, and a short pry beak. It was built for boarding windows fast, not for tidy carpentry.
A compact roll of cloth patches, tarp squares, spare cord, and needles for keeping bedrolls, tents, and packs alive.
A wire-and-wood crate sized for bottles, jars, and small tools. Awkward, but stable.
A roll of padded wraps and separators for keeping carried bottles from clacking together.
A pierced bucket with cloth and mesh inserts for separating grit, glass, and sludge from scavenged water or soil.
A crude purifier made from stacked cans, cloth, gravel, and charcoal. It is slow and bulky, but it can make suspect water drinkable when proper treatment gear is gone.
A chunky opener and punch for dented cans, improvised vents, and ugly sheet-metal holes.
Nested cans, wire supports, and a soot-black wind shield. Slow, but it turns scraps of fuel and patience into a meal.
A lidded tin packed for carrying filter charcoal without smearing black dust over everything.
Two smooth blocks and a strap clamp for flattening patched cloth and glued liners.
A folded sheet-metal box that shields a small cooking fire from wind and keeps coals off wet ground.
A row of dull teeth mounted in scrap wood, used to strip fibers and straighten salvaged thread.
A forked stick with a crank and notches for twisting fibers and scraps into usable cord.
A roll of bent wire, shaved scrap, and tension pieces. It is not a proper locksmith kit, but it gives you a chance against simple locks without smashing the frame.
A stiff salvaged broom head reinforced with wire for clearing glass, ash, and loose nails.
Several wooden wedges and a small metal shim tied together. Good for holding doors, jamming loose gates, or marking a room as temporarily safe.
A handful of reflective strips, chalk, and cloth tags for marking cleared rooms, bad stairwells, cached supplies, and doors you never want to open again.
A heavy needle, awl point, and thread spool mounted into a palm handle.
A tarp with peg loops and stitched drain corners for processing carcasses without mixing meat, mud, and glass.
A dented pot with a wire bail, lid holes, and a soot-blackened bottom. It is good enough for boiling water, rendering scraps, and pretending dinner is still a normal thing.
A small punch-and-anvil kit for setting holes in tarp, cloth, and soft leather.
A flat board used as a backing plate for sewing and gluing patches into ruined cloth.
Needles, thread, spare buttons, leather scraps, and a small awl wrapped in waxed cloth. It is meant for keeping gear wearable after fences, teeth, and bad luck tear it open.
A bucket fitted with a cloth strainer and hanging handle. For rinsing bandages, washing grit off hands, or making dirty water slightly less horrifying.
A fire axe with a pipe extension and reinforced head. It is too awkward for a firehouse manual, but excellent for hooking things you do not want close.
Two light poles, cloth, and straps arranged into a crude carry litter for moving supplies or a person who is not arguing much.
A hinged set of thin boards and straps that can become a rigid arm or leg support.
A stiff rag broom reinforced with wire for pushing broken glass aside without kneeling in it.
A chalky cloth marker and tape tabs for marking cleared paths through broken windows and debris fields.
A small clamp made from scrap and a screw handle. It holds parts badly, which is still better than holding them with your knees.
A light frame and strap cradle for carrying bottles or small jugs without destroying your hands.
Flat sticks, cloth strips, and tape bundled for emergency limb support. It is more field improvisation than medicine, but it belongs in any serious go-bag.
A thin, flat scraper for cleaning boots, wheels, drains, and corpse muck from places nobody should touch.
A narrow bar ground into a hooked nail puller. It ruins boards more slowly than a crowbar and fingers less often than pliers.
A heavy pry-and-punch bar meant to split wall panels, crate lids, and cheap doors.
Several little clamps made from bolts, wood, and bent steel. Useful when glue, stitching, or sanity needs something held still.
A small hand crimper made from pliers, filed notches, and a tightening screw.
Pre-cut wire, cord, straps, and toggles packed for fastening gear under pressure.
A shortened prybar wrapped in leather and cloth to muffle knocks against frames. It is still not truly quiet, but it is less stupid than raw steel on steel.

A battered multitool rebuilt for post-Cataclysm scavenging: pliers, a short blade, a flat driver, a small saw, and a glass-breaking nub. It is not elegant, but it covers the common problems of locked sheds, stalled engines, and ruined furniture.
A long hook made from pipe and scrap, meant for dragging loose debris or snagging reachable objects from unsafe ledges.
A short-handled hoe made for pulling loose rubble, corpse ash, and broken glass toward you before stepping into a room.
A light metal rod with marked tape, a hooked end, and a blunt tip. It is for checking floor voids, snagging straps, and disturbing debris before you put your hand somewhere stupid.
A dead-blow style mallet made from a pipe sleeve, rags, and packed scrap.
A wooden frame with wire mesh and cloth ties, useful for shaking dirt, ash, glass, and small rubble away from useful fragments.
Long tongs hammered from thin steel, useful for pulling hot cans from fires and dragging sharp debris without losing fingers.
A compact reel of recovered cord with a crude brake, useful for marking routes and tying temporary pulls.
A heavy sawblade section ground into a cleaver and lashed to a handle. It bites brutally, but the edge is crude and the balance is hateful.
A sharpened scrap-metal blade with a tape-and-rag handle. It is better than bare hands, useful for rough cutting, and disposable enough that no one worries about keeping it pretty.
A slow hand drill assembled from a driver handle, scavenged gears, and a sharpened bit. It is ugly, but it can start holes in wood, plastic, and thin sheet metal.
A battered level repaired with plastic and tape. It helps when doors, frames, and rain collectors need to be less crooked than the world.
A broad strip of sharpened spring steel with a bolted grip. It is rougher than a factory machete but dangerous in the same familiar way.
A worn file with a wrapped handle, still good for taking burrs off sharpened scrap.
A short hatchet rebuilt from a cracked head and emergency tape. Useful for kindling and ugly fights.
A shortened wood saw with a protected grip, made for tight rooms and broken furniture.
A bent scrap whistle on a cord. Loud enough to bring help, dead things, or both.
A striker and ferro rod mounted into a taped scrap handle. It is reliable enough to matter, but starting a fire in bad weather still takes patience and tinder.
A tarp with reinforced corners, cord loops, and a wrapped drainage point. It is not machinery; it is just the sort of simple water trick people remember after the taps die.
A thin hooked scraper for clearing lint, ash, and gunk from vents, grates, and narrow gaps.
A shovel with reinforced edges, a taped grip, and extra metal at the back of the blade. It still digs, but now it also solves arguments.
A palm-sized spring punch rebuilt from salvaged hardware. It is meant for cracking vehicle glass and working small latches, but it can serve as a desperate fist-load in close quarters.
A hooked strip of scrap on a wooden handle for clearing glass from frames before climbing through.
A braided wire saw with rag-wrapped finger loops. It folds tiny, cuts slowly, and ruins your hands if you rush it, but it can process branches when real saws are gone.
A slotted driver for twisting wire ties tight around splints, plates, and barricade lashings.
A bulky cloth mask with charcoal discs and a nose bridge cut from thin scrap.
A bicycle helmet fitted with a scratched plastic face visor and extra cloth padding. It is a compromise between crash protection and not getting spit in the eyes.
A padded collar stiffened with scrap strips to protect the neck from grabbing teeth.
Work gloves reinforced with glassproof panels, leather wrap, and scrap knuckle plates.
Rubber and leather spats strapped over boots to keep glass, gore, and water out of the laces.
A strap rig with padded loops for jars, bottles, and small tins. It rattles unless packed carefully.
A tight harness with padded bottle sleeves that keeps water weight close to the ribs.
A webbing harness with recovered buckles and small pockets for light tools.
A heavy scavenger pack with a stiff frame and enough tie points to make bad decisions easier.
A coat patched with canvas panels, tarp flaps, and ugly stitching. It sheds light rain and most splinters.
A soft hood stiffened with a strip of scrap over the brow and sides.
A cut-down hood, bandana, and plastic eye shield sewn into one ugly head covering for ash, wind, and corpse-stink dust.
A duffel bag rebuilt with backpack straps and extra lash points. It carries a lot and handles like a sack of bricks.
A compact medical bag reinforced with tarp, glove loops, and color-marked pockets. It is sized for running, kneeling, and not dumping bandages into the mud.
A stained smock with taped cuffs, cloth pockets, and enough coverage to handle blood, dust, and panic.
A dust hood with a charcoal filter pack sewn around the mouth and extra cloth over the brow.
A long scarf with stitched-in cloth filter discs. Mostly comfort, partly protection.
A layered cloth mouth-wrap packed with charcoal dust and held tight with elastic. It will not replace a real gas mask, but it helps against ash, dust, smoke, and the foul grit kicked up by the dead.
A layered wool-and-nomex shoulder wrap for sparks, cold watches, and sleeping too close to a barrel fire.
A wider, tougher tarp poncho with repair panels and grommet ties.
Forearm wraps with plastic and denim layers, made for reaching through broken windows and ruined shelves.
Layers of towel, tape, and leather wrapped around the palms for crawling through windows and handling jagged scrap. They make fine work worse but save blood.
Stiff gaiters built from tarp, rubber strips, and mesh to protect lower legs from broken glass.
A heavy cloth jacket quilted from blankets, towels, and thread. Medieval logic works fine when the enemy mostly bites and bludgeons.
A dented pot with a liner and chin strap. Dumb-looking, better than skull fracture.
Knee guards and shin wraps built to protect against low crawlers and broken furniture.
A heavy belt and strap rig for tools, rope, and climbing junk. It pinches when overloaded; it is always overloaded.
A heavy satchel rebuilt with salvage straps and a stiff bottom panel.
A close-fitting padded collar with hidden scrap plates around the throat.
A thick work apron reinforced with plates and canvas over the stomach and ribs.
A strip of mesh and cloth worn over the eyes. Visibility suffers; glass suffers more.
A thick cloth mask packed with filter cloth and charcoal. Good for dust and bad smells, not miracles.
Thick sleeves made from jacket arms, towels, and leather patches. They are hot and clumsy, but they give grabbing corpses something less important to chew.
A strap system meant to make improvised heavy packs slightly less miserable.
Heavy detachable sleeves made from quilted padding and glassproof patches.
A tool apron with rows of tiny pockets for fasteners, bits, and whatever you swear you just had.
Forearm guards made from bent scrap panels and salvage straps.
A hoodie lined with small scrap plates and padding where bites usually land.
Clumsy mitts with plate over the back of the hand, made for breaking glass and surviving grabs.
A long coat reinforced with bent plate panels at the ribs, shoulders, and forearms. It carries weight like a bad memory.
Cargo pants rebuilt with quilted liners and scrap plates over the thighs.
A wearable blanket-cloak with ties and a hood. It smells like old smoke and buys warmth cheaply.
A wearable sleeping wrap patched from blankets and tarp. Warm, bulky, and better than shivering.
A stitched layer of blankets, towels, and padding worn under rough armor. It softens impacts and prevents chafing, though it gets hot fast.
A tarp cut into a hooded poncho, tied with cord and reinforced at the shoulders. Loud, sweaty, and very welcome during filthy weather.

A heavy jacket patched with leather, taped seams, and thin scrap plates over the shoulders and ribs. It keeps the familiar utility of a pocketed coat while giving a little more confidence around broken glass and grabbing hands.
A harness of bright straps, small pouches, and clipped loops for gloves, masks, bandages, and a radio. It looks official from a distance, which is less useful now.
Knee guards patched together from plastic shell, rubber strips, and salvage straps.
A cracked plastic visor and mesh guard bolted to straps.
A patched cloak with a tarp outer and quiet inner cloth, made for bad weather and worse rooftops.
Rubber and tarp wraps strapped around boots to keep water and glass out.
Layered shoulder pads made from scrap guard plates and folded tarp.
A compact pack with tight straps and protected pockets, meant for moving fast through buildings without snagging every door.
A thick leather-and-denim apron with a few small tool loops. Useful at a workbench, tolerable in a fight, and covered in stains nobody wants identified.
A compact daypack rebuilt with a tarp liner, zipper strips, and a few quiet lash points.
A crude frame pack made from rods, tarp, and straps. It carries awkward loot and punishes your shoulders for asking.
A backpack reinforced with tarp panels, extra stitching, and a crude frame. It carries more than it should, which is usually how bad decisions start.
Work gloves with extra leather over the fingers and small steel strips sewn across the knuckles. They make fine work clumsy, but they are better than bare skin in a ruin.
Safety goggles patched with tape, foam, and scratched plastic. They are ugly, foggy, and better than glass in the eyes.
Cargo trousers patched with denim, leather, and small plates over the thighs and knees. The pockets are still useful; the stitching is less trustworthy.
A belt covered in quiet pouches for tinder, lockpicks, snacks, and bad decisions.
Forearm guards made from flattened cans, leather, and tape. They scrape doorframes constantly, which is better than losing skin.
Metal and leather caps strapped over ordinary boots. They are ugly, heavy, and reassuring when broken glass is everywhere.
A sports chestguard reinforced with sheet metal, leather scraps, and ugly rows of tape. It is uncomfortable and noisy, but it spreads bites and blunt blows across the torso.
A bent mesh and plastic faceguard tied over the lower face. It interferes with comfort, but it keeps broken fingers and snapping teeth off your mouth.
A hard hat with extra padding, a wire chin strap, and a few small plates riveted where someone expected falling debris or biting jaws.
Overlapping scrap strips hanging from a belt to protect hips and thighs.
Overlapping scrap strips laced onto a leather vest. Flexible enough to wear, loud enough to announce regret.
A short mantle of leather, cloth, and scrap plates hanging over the shoulders and upper chest.
Low-profile shin guards made from split pipe, straps, and padding.
Improvised shin guards built from sports pads, hard plastic, and strips of metal. Good against bites near the floor; bad for walking quietly.
A stained apron reinforced with tarred patches and glove loops. Built for boiling, not beauty.
A pair of asymmetric shoulder plates mounted on straps, useful against bites from above and doorway grabs.
Heavy trousers lined with baffle panels where debris usually cuts.
Work gloves reinforced with scrap plates over the knuckles and padded palms.
Loose waterproof overmitts that keep muck and glass out at the cost of dexterity.
A stiff tarred cloth cape that turns rain and attracts grime in equal measure.
A torso vest made from cut tire tread, belts, and cloth padding. It smells awful and moves worse, but it shrugs off bites and scrape wounds surprisingly well.
A strap rig with loops for small hand tools, blades, and fasteners. It is not armor, but it keeps work close enough to matter.
A belt with reinforced loops and shallow pouches for tiny tools and fasteners.
Boots reinforced with tire tread patches, leather, and toe caps. Heavy, grippy, and not subtle.
A cross-body sling made from belts and small pouches. It keeps light tools close without committing to a full pack.
A padded sling made to carry heavy bottles tight against the body.
A crude cage of wire and thin bar stock strapped over the face. It restricts vision but keeps fingers and teeth off skin.
Thick sleeves and mesh patches worn over the forearms while climbing through broken windows.
A cloth hood with wire mesh over the face to stop glass and grabbing fingers.
A civilian fortification booklet about leverage, bracing, dead space, door swings, window boards, and false security.
A grim training booklet on keeping dead hands at the far end of sticks, hooks, and doorways.
Notes on pulling useful material from collapsed rooms without burying yourself in them.

name.str_pl: copies of Cloth, Plate, and Tape

description: A practical tailoring booklet focused on padding, straps, rough repairs, and wearable protection. Half the margins are warnings about heat rash and bad stitching.

name.str_pl: copies of Cloth Filters and Masks

description: A practical guide to layered cloth, charcoal, dust masks, and the limits of improvised filtration.
A grim pamphlet about reach, footing, doorways, and why most improvised weapons fail when people swing too hard.
A tactical-looking civilian booklet about entering rooms already occupied by corpses.
A shelter pamphlet about splints, bleeding, dust, and moving injured people through cluttered spaces.

name.str_pl: copies of Emergency Cookery Without Power

description: A pragmatic booklet on boiling, batch water handling, safe containers, tiny stoves, and making bland food less miserable.
A notebook of field fixes using hinges, buckles, straps, grommets, and drilled scrap.

name.str_pl: copies of Field Fortifications for Civilians

description: A sober little guide to boarding windows, making noise traps, choosing defensible rooms, and avoiding the false confidence of a bad barricade.
A shelter manual focused on drinkable water, small fires, sleeping cold, rain catches, and not burning down your only room.
A rough tailor-fabricator guide to padding, lamellar strips, strap points, heat buildup, and the difference between armor and junk tied to your body.
A stained garage notebook about temporary fixes, clamps, straps, field drilling, and repairs that only have to survive one more trip.
A thin pamphlet on lock types, improvised picks, bypass tricks, and why breaking a door is sometimes quieter than doing a bad job slowly.
A garage manual about cold bending, filing, drilling, and making scrap plates less lethal to the wearer.
Handwritten notes on load distribution, pouch placement, strap repair, and why one giant bag ruins knees.
A short guide to lock approaches, pry angles, jammed doors, window clearing, and when to walk away.

name.str_pl: copies of Rain and Ash Camping

description: A post-storm camping guide rewritten in pencil by someone who survived worse weather than forecasts predicted.

name.str_pl: copies of Rooftop and Window Work

description: A terse manual about ladders, windows, glass, ledges, and why falling is quieter than screaming only once.
A practical manual about moving through houses, back rooms, roofs, windows, and alleys without turning every search into a fight.
Field notes on splints, bandage reuse, dirty water, shock, and the ugly compromises of medicine after supply chains die.

name.str_pl: copies of Scrapcraft for Bad Days

description: A spiral-bound collection of ugly but functional projects: pry tools, patched handles, reinforced packs, and the kind of craft that starts with broken furniture.
A survivor-made pack design guide: straps, balance, lash points, and what your spine can tolerate.
A dirty notebook filled with shelter advice, scavenging checklists, water cautions, and hard-learned rules written by several increasingly tired hands.
Laminated cards describing bleeding control, splints, shock, burns, and when to stop pretending you have enough supplies.
